claim: The Currier A/B dialect split reflects two scribal hands encoding the same underlying language using different homophonic substitution tables, where Currier A substitutes fewer glyphs per phoneme than Currier B, explaining B's larger word count (23,766 vs 11,022) via greater glyph variety per syllable.
evidence_for: Currier B has more than twice the word count of A, suggesting richer or more verbose encoding
Homophonic ciphers are period-appropriate hand-performable techniques consistent with the manuscript's era (~1400-1438)
The overall Zipf exponent (0.8946) is slightly below natural language, consistent with homophonic expansion inflating rare word forms
Different sections show different entropies, consistent with different homophonic table densities
evidence_against: Glyph entropy (3.8627) is below what a dense homophonic cipher would typically produce (homophones increase entropy toward the theoretical maximum)
The hypothesis of two languages (H004, H008) is currently rated more highly (0.774, 0.866); homophonic same-language encoding is competing with that
Only 25 unique glyphs constrains the homophonic table size significantly
test: Compute bigram transition entropy separately for Currier A and B corpora. If homophonic tables differ, A and B should show statistically different bigram distributions (KL divergence > threshold) but similar unigram entropy if encoding the same language.
test_metric: KL divergence between Currier A and B bigram transition matrices; per-corpus unigram glyph entropy
test_threshold: KL divergence > 0.3 nats between A and B bigram matrices with unigram entropy difference < 0.1 bits would support same-language different-cipher-table hypothesis; reverse pattern supports different-language hypothesis

tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.
